provenance.title: Reinforcement Learning for Optimized Trade Execution
provenance.abstract: We present the first large-scale empirical application of reinforcement learning to the important problem of optimized trade execution in modern financial markets. Our experiments are based on 1.5 years of millisecond time-scale limit order data from NASDAQ.
key_claims: RL can improve relative performance over submit-and-leave strategies by up to 50%
Algorithm exploits approximate independence between private (time, inventory) and market variables for computational efficiency
First large-scale empirical RL application to trade execution using microstructure data
Learned policies are interpretable: more aggressive pricing when time is short and inventory is high
Market variables (bid-ask spread, immediate market order cost) add further improvement over private-variable-only policies

architecture.primary_variant: Q-learning + Dynamic Programming hybrid with private/market state factorization

modules.name: StateRepresentation
modules.role: Encode current world state from private and market variables

modules.name: ActionSpace
modules.role: Define limit order price offset relative to ask/bid
modules.note: Positive a = cross spread toward buyers; negative = deeper in own book

modules.name: MarketSimulator
modules.role: Simulate order execution against historical INET order book data

modules.name: QTableUpdater
modules.role: Maintain and update Q-value table c(x, a) via incremental averaging

modules.name: PolicyExtractor
modules.role: For each state select argmin-cost action from Q-table
inputs: Q-table c(x, a) for all states

variants.name: PrivateOnly
variants.description: State = <t, i> only; no market variables

variants.name: WithMarketVars
variants.description: State = <t, i, o1...oR>; R market variables discretized to low-resolution buckets

mathematical_spec.name: Q-value (cost) update rule
mathematical_spec.latex: c(x, a) = \frac{n}{n+1} c(x, a) + \frac{1}{n+1} \left[ c_{im}(x, a) + \arg\max_p c(y, p) \right]
mathematical_spec.role: Incremental averaging update for expected execution cost; fuses Q-learning with dynamic programming

mathematical_spec.name: Limit order action (sell side)
mathematical_spec.role: Maps integer action to actual order book price offset

mathematical_spec.name: Mid-spread baseline
mathematical_spec.role: Reference price for measuring trading cost

mathematical_spec.name: Trading cost (basis points)
mathematical_spec.role: Primary performance metric; underperformance vs mid-spread baseline in basis points

mathematical_spec.name: Data complexity (backward induction passes)
mathematical_spec.role: Total number of dataset traversals required for training; independent of number of market variables R

tensor_semantics.name: state_vector
tensor_semantics.description: Concatenation of private vars (t, i) and R market variables

tensor_semantics.name: q_table
tensor_semantics.description: Full state-action cost table; stores expected execution cost for each (state, action) pair
tensor_semantics.role: central data structure; read and written during training; read-only at inference

tensor_semantics.name: action
tensor_semantics.description: Discrete price offset in ticks relative to ask (sell) or bid (buy)

training_pipeline.effective_batch_size: One order book snapshot per update step
training_pipeline.training_steps: ~45,000 episodes per stock per H=2min setting (1 year of data)
training_pipeline.epochs: T * I * L passes over training data
training_pipeline.curriculum: Backward induction: train from t=T down to t=0
training_pipeline.notes: Algorithm is table-based, not neural. No gradient descent. Learning via incremental averaging of realized costs.

datasets.name: INET ECN NASDAQ historical order book data

metrics.name: Trading cost (basis points)
metrics.description: Underperformance vs mid-spread at episode start; lower is better

reported_results.condition: RL with private + market variables vs S&L

baselines: Submit-and-Leave (S&L) with optimized fixed limit price
special_conditions: Test set simulations fully account for market impact of policy actions (independence assumption used only in training)
Experiments run for V in {5000, 10000} shares and H in {2, 8} minutes

implementation_assumptions.assumption: Q-table stored as dense numpy array indexed by (t, i, discretized_market_vars, action)
implementation_assumptions.basis: Standard tabular RL; paper describes discrete state and action spaces

implementation_assumptions.assumption: Market variables discretized into 3 bins (0=low, 1=medium, 2=high) unless stated otherwise
implementation_assumptions.basis: Explicitly stated in Section 5 for transaction volume; implied for others

implementation_assumptions.assumption: Action space L = ~15–20 discrete price offsets (e.g. -6 to +14 ticks based on Figure 5 x-axis)
implementation_assumptions.basis: Inferred from Figure 5 and Figure 7 x-axis ranges (-6 to +14)

implementation_assumptions.assumption: Episode partitioning is non-overlapping sliding windows of length H over the trading day
implementation_assumptions.basis: Paper states ~45,000 episodes from 1-year data at H=2min; trading day ~6.5h → ~195 episodes/day × 252 days ≈ 49,140

implementation_assumptions.assumption: Order book data loaded into memory per episode; no lookahead beyond episode boundary
Framework: Python with NumPy/Pandas for data handling; no neural networks required
implementation_assumptions.basis: Tabular RL algorithm; 2006 paper predates modern DL frameworks

ambiguities.description: Exact number of discrete actions L is not stated explicitly; inferred from figures
Discretization bins for each market variable not fully specified; only transaction volume is explicitly described as 3-level
Exact reward signal formulation (immediate payout per step vs terminal only) is described qualitatively; precise normalization not stated
ambiguities.primary_assumption: Cumulative sum of per-step execution proceeds minus mid-spread value of executed shares
